---
date: "2026-06-29"
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD"
---

## Measurement Error and Attenuation Toward Zero

- Part 5 — measurement error and attenuation toward zero

::: notes
Welcome back to Chapter two-point-five, part five of nine. In part four we learned the two-sign rule and saw that adding controls is only a bet. Now the second threat, which strikes even when you have omitted nothing at all: measurement error. The result, in one sentence — when your key regressor is a noisy version of the true variable, with noise that is just random static, the OLS coefficient is biased toward zero. That is attenuation bias, and the shrinkage factor is the share of your regressor's variance that is real signal rather than noise.
:::

## Classical Errors-in-Variables

- True model uses true regressor: $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x^* + \varepsilon$
- You observe a noisy copy: $x = x^* + m$
- CEV: $m$ mean zero, uncorrelated with $x^*$ and with $\varepsilon$
- New error $u = \varepsilon - \beta_1 m$ shares $m$ with $x$ — mechanically correlated

::: notes
Set it up. The true model uses the true regressor x-star: y equals beta-zero plus beta-one x-star plus epsilon. But you do not observe x-star. You observe a mismeasured version, x equals x-star plus m, where m is measurement error. The classical errors-in-variables assumptions say the error is pure random static: m has mean zero, is uncorrelated with the true value x-star, and is uncorrelated with the equation error epsilon. Think of m as a thermometer reading the true temperature plus an independent jiggle. Now substitute x-star equals x minus m into the true model. You get y equals beta-zero plus beta-one x plus a new composite error, epsilon minus beta-one times m. Look at that new error: it contains negative beta-one times m. And the regressor x equals x-star plus m also contains m. So the regressor and the error share the term m — they are mechanically correlated. The zero-conditional-mean assumption fails again, this time because the noise in our regressor leaks into the error.
:::

## The Attenuation Factor

$$\hat{\beta}_1 \xrightarrow{p} \beta_1 \cdot \underbrace{\frac{\sigma_{x^*}^2}{\sigma_{x^*}^2 + \sigma_m^2}}_{\lambda}$$

- $\operatorname{Cov}(x,y) = \beta_1\sigma_{x^*}^2$; $\operatorname{Var}(x) = \sigma_{x^*}^2 + \sigma_m^2$
- $\lambda$ lies strictly between 0 and 1 — pulls the estimate **toward zero**
- $\lambda$ = **reliability ratio**: fraction of variance that is real signal

::: notes
Now compute the consequence. The probability limit of the OLS slope is the covariance of x and y over the variance of x. Under the classical assumptions, the numerator is beta-one times the variance of x-star, because m is uncorrelated with both x-star and epsilon, killing every cross term. The denominator splits cleanly into the variance of x-star plus the variance of m. Divide, and the slope converges not to beta-one but to beta-one times a factor lambda, which equals the variance of x-star over the variance of x-star plus the variance of m. Because variances are non-negative, lambda lies strictly between zero and one whenever there is any noise. So the estimate is pulled toward zero — smaller in magnitude than the truth, regardless of sign. That is attenuation bias. And lambda has a beautiful name: the reliability ratio, the fraction of your observed regressor's variance that is genuine signal. If half the variance is noise, lambda is one-half and your coefficient is exactly half its true size. Unlike omitted variable bias, the direction here is always known — toward zero. A real effect looks weaker than it is, and you may miss a relationship that genuinely exists. For Maya, self-reported income is exactly this: noisy, so its coefficient understates how much true income matters for approval.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Classical noise in your regressor multiplies $\hat\beta_1$ by the reliability ratio $\lambda \in (0,1)$ — **attenuation toward zero**. The direction is always known, and more data never fixes it.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part five: classical measurement error in your regressor multiplies the coefficient by the reliability ratio lambda, a number strictly between zero and one, pulling the estimate toward zero — attenuation. Half the variance being noise halves your coefficient. Unlike omitted variable bias, the direction is always known: toward zero, so a real effect looks weaker than it is. In part six we cover three subtleties that trip people up — why noise in the outcome is harmless, why a noisy control re-opens omitted variable bias, and how non-classical error can push away from zero — and then we watch attenuation slide a coefficient toward zero in live code.
:::
